content:
{
  "title": "Prompt Sanitization via Adversarial Pattern Masking for Robust LLM Generation",
  "problem": "Large language models are susceptible to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that elicit unsafe or undesired outputs. Current prompting methods do not actively mitigate adversarial patterns at inference time.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in sensitive domains, robustness against prompt-based attacks becomes critical. Recent jailbreak techniques exploit prompt structures undetectable by static filters, highlighting the need for dynamic, adaptive defenses. Prompting methods that can actively sanitize or neutralize adversarial content are timely and necessary.",
  "method": "We propose a prompt sanitization technique that dynamically masks or rephrases detected adversarial patterns within user prompts before LLM processing. A lightweight, trainable adversarial pattern detector is prepended to the LLM pipeline, flagging suspicious n-gram or structural signatures. Upon detection, a masking and rewriting module minimally alters the prompt (e.g., by replacing trigger phrases or randomizing exploitable syntax), preserving semantics while disrupting attack vectors. The modified prompt is then provided to the LLM for final generation, blocking common jailbreak bypasses.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on benchmark adversarial and jailbreak prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) using GPT-4/3.5 as base models. Baselines include unmodified prompting, static keyword filtering, and recent adversarial training methods. Metrics include attack success rate, output utility (BLEU, ROUGE), and prompt semantic preservation (BERTScore). Human evaluation will further assess safety and faithfulness of outputs."
}
